result: Fog folds the city like origami, turning streetlights into dim lanterns hung above invisible rivers of asphalt. I walk beneath them pretending each is a checkpoint in a quiet pilgrimage toward some unnamed center. Somewhere a cathedral bell rehearses the hour, but the mist swallows its brass tongue; the sound arrives as rumor, a tremor in the cartilage of the ribs. I think of how many voices I’ve swallowed trying to speak for someone else— borrowed metaphors, rental grief, all the pronouns that never quite fit. Tonight I return them to the vapor. Tonight I allow myself one syllable without precedent: the shape of breath curling back into the mouth that released it. Inside that small weather I discover an even smaller room: a childhood kitchen where the radio played only songs in minor keys, where my grandmother stirred lentils into solar eclipses, humming off-key protection against whatever tomorrow threatened. Decades later she is ash, the station is static, yet the aroma survives like a refugee, boarding the body without papers. I inhale until my lungs remember they are also ovens, also gramophones, also lanterns. Then exhale: a slow freight of atomized memory rising to join the fog. City and self remix into one gray manuscript. No margins. No title. Only the ongoing sentence of footsteps that never quite end, they just fade into someone else’s paragraph, someone else’s indefinite, insistent becoming.
